rendered_brief:
OTIF ROOT-CAUSE BOARD BRIEF

Scope: 140 failed orders with combined order value of AED 1,280,297.

RANKED DRIVERS BY CASH AT RISK

supplier — AED 526,211 (41% of total, 50 orders, 36% of failures). Supplier lateness clustered across a wide range of inbound lead-time overruns, leaving insufficient on-hand stock at pick time. In a subset of contested orders, late purchase-order volumes alone exceeded the resulting shortfall, confirming that on-time delivery would have prevented the failure outright. Action: enforce a tiered supplier scorecard with financial penalties triggered at defined lateness thresholds, and mandate safety-stock buffers sized to the observed lead-time variance for the highest-risk vendors.

demand — AED 331,548 (26% of total, 36 orders, 26% of failures). Two distinct fault modes were identified: dispatch delays where fully picked orders sat in the warehouse beyond the promised ship date, and short-pick errors where stock was physically available yet fewer units were released than on hand. Both modes are internal execution failures with no upstream dependency. Action: implement a same-day dispatch SLA with automated escalation alerts and introduce a dual-scan pick-verification step to close the gap between system-recorded inventory and units physically released.

warehouse — AED 281,764 (22% of total, 31 orders, 22% of failures). Forecast errors were consistently large and positively biased — actual demand materially exceeded plan in every attributed order — driving stock-outs at pick with no upstream replenishment buffer to absorb the surge. Action: adopt a rolling short-cycle forecast refresh cadence using point-of-sale signals and apply statistical safety-stock uplift proportional to observed forecast error volatility by SKU.

logistics — AED 140,774 (11% of total, 23 orders, 16% of failures). Transit overages above lane SLA were the sole cause in the majority of logistics-attributed orders; a smaller subset also carried a warehouse dispatch delay that compounded the transit overage. In all cases goods were fully picked and correctly dispatched before the carrier failure occurred. Action: renegotiate carrier SLAs with embedded service-credit clauses and activate real-time shipment-tracking alerts to trigger proactive customer communication when transit overage risk is detected mid-journey.

WHAT COULD NOT BE ESTABLISHED

For orders where both demand and supplier links fired simultaneously, the investigation could not isolate a single root cause with certainty; the attribution reflects the dominant signal (forecast-miss magnitude versus PO coverage ratio) but a combined demand-and-supply failure cannot be ruled out in those cases. Additionally, the analysis does not capture revenue lost from orders that were cancelled before entry or from customers who did not reorder after a service failure, so the true cash impact is likely understated relative to AED 1,280,297.
